behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as deletion + addition
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on real git's rename detection mechanism, which requires executing git as a subprocess with correct command flags (e.g., --name-status or --raw). A mock of git output could pass even if the tool calls git with wrong flags, or if it misparsels the real rename output format. Real git's rename detection also varies by version and configuration. Only subprocess execution against real git repos will catch failures where the tool doesn't properly invoke or parse git's rename format.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test git repository with an initial file, rename the file in a commit, then run the tool and verify the renamed file appears only in the 'renamed' category, not counted as both a deletion and an addition. Also verify the counts for additions and deletions don't include the renamed file.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output
behaviors.justification: Real binary files can trigger crashes that sanitized mocks would hide—null bytes, large file sizes, or specific file formats in actual git output. Git's handling of binary files is OS and version-dependent, and the tool's robustness against edge cases in binary data can only be verified by executing git against real binary files on the filesystem. A mock that just returns '{binary file}' would not expose parsing errors or crashes triggered by actual binary data.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test git repository, commit multiple binary files (image files, compiled objects), run the tool, verify it completes without crashing and that the binary files are counted in the 'added', 'modified', or 'deleted' category as appropriate. Test with at least two different binary file formats.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error
behaviors.justification: An empty git repository is a specific edge case where git produces its own error messages or behaviors. The exact format and content of git's output on an empty repo is version-dependent, and the tool could crash on the real error message format. A mock that returns a clean 'no commits' response might not trigger the crash that the real git error would. Only subprocess execution against a real empty repository will confirm the tool handles this edge case correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a new git repository with no commits, run the tool, verify it produces a valid empty report (e.g., empty JSON or empty counts) without exiting with an error code or raising an exception.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed
behaviors.justification: This is the core correctness behavior and requires testing against real git repositories with complex histories. A mock of git output would hide parsing bugs by definition—you'd only be testing against your own mock expectations, not against real git's format. Real failures could arise from edge cases in parsing, handling of merge commits, file path encoding, or other git output format subtleties. The tool's counting logic must be validated against real git's actual output across diverse repository histories.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test repository with a sequence of commits that include: pure file additions, pure file deletions, file modifications, file renames, and mixed operations in single commits. Run the tool and verify that the 'added', 'deleted', 'renamed', and 'modified' counts match the actual changes observable via git log and git diff. Include edge cases like files with spaces or special characters in names.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged
behaviors.justification: This behavior tests that the tool uses the correct git command flags (e.g., git diff HEAD vs git diff) to analyze changes regardless of staging status. The tool must be calling git with the right flags to capture all changes regardless of their staging state. A mock of git output would hide the failure mode where the tool calls git with the wrong flags and only sees staged or only sees unstaged changes. Real git staging state can only be verified by subprocess execution against a real repository with staged and unstaged changes.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test repository with a baseline commit, make a modification to a file, stage the change, run the tool and record output, then unstage the change, run the tool again and record output, and verify both outputs are identical. Repeat with file additions and deletions. This confirms the tool uses git commands that show all changes regardless of staging status.
